director.summary: Week 26 shows mixed performance across the estate with significant divergence between stores. Skerries delivers strong growth (+9.3% YTD, +0.8% weekly) driven by summer tourism and organic produce strength, while Balbriggan struggles (-2.97% YTD, -3.8% weekly) with broad-based weakness in impulse, edible grocery, and personal care. Palmerstown is essentially flat (+0.05% weekly) despite strong deli and meat performance, held back by weakness in packaged bakery and beers/wines. Calendar effects (Easter shifted to late March 2025) and early summer seasonality are influencing patterns, but execution gaps in margin management are visible across all three stores.

stores.name: Palmerstown
stores.summary: Flat weekly sales (+0.05%) mask strong departmental performance in deli (+20.7%), meat (+10.4%), and produce (+7.9%), offset by sharp declines in beers/wines (-7.5%), baby & kids (-23.2%), and packaged bakery (-25.7%). Margins compressed -0.34% despite volume lifts, signalling pricing or mix pressure. Lunchtime commuter trade remains solid but impulse performance is soft.

stores.name: Balbriggan
stores.summary: Significant underperformance with sales down -3.8% weekly and -2.97% YTD. All major grocery categories declining; impulse -3.8%, edible -5.6%, personal care -12.1%. Fish serveover, lamb, pork, and packaged cake all materially weak. Only bright spots are deli (+27.8%) and fresh bakery (+22.5%), insufficient to offset widespread softness. This is a store in need of tactical intervention.

stores.name: Skerries
stores.summary: Clear outperformer with +9.3% YTD growth and +0.8% weekly sales. Produce exceptionally strong (+9.2%), organic +45.9%, and prepared produce +12.1% suggesting strong tourist/affluent demographic engagement. Chicken serveover +26.4%, deli +3.9% (vs. peers), and impulse +12.2%. Waste control good at 1.1%, but margin erosion -0.56% requires attention despite volume gains.

drivers.text: Skerries produce outperformance (+9.2% weekly, organic +45.9%) indicates strong seasonal uptick and affluent customer base responding to quality positioning.
Cross-store deli strength (Palmerstown +20.7%, Balbriggan +27.8%, Skerries +3.9%) driven by hot food (+27.4%, +13.5%, -4.3% respectively) is capturing warm weather demand.
Balbriggan -3.8% weekly decline is systemic: impulse, edible grocery, non-food, baby/kids, and personal care all contracting. No compensating strengths sufficient to offset.
Margin compression across all stores (-0.34%, -0.49%, -0.56%) despite volume growth suggests aggressive promotions, mix degradation, or waste creep; beers/wines margins particularly weak.
Fish serveover down sharply across all stores (Palmerstown -17.3%, Balbriggan -7.0%, Skerries -23.5%); seasonal rotation expected but magnitude suggests potential supply/display issues.

Packaged bakery decline across portfolio (Palmerstown -25.7%, Balbriggan -27.4%, Skerries -2.9%) is disproportionate to fresh bakery growth, indicating potential shelf positioning or promotional conflict.

priorities: Diagnose Balbriggan underperformance: execute targeted basket review, validate supply/availability, check promotional calendar alignment with Palmerstown/Skerries.
Address margin erosion across all stores: audit promotional intensity, review waste/reduction data (Balbriggan 1.34% dairy waste vs. Palmerstown 1.21%), benchmark pricing strategy.
Stabilise fish counter performance: review staff scheduling, product rotation, and cross-merchandising opportunities (pairing with prepared/fresh produce).
Capitalise on Skerries summer momentum: increase tourist-targeted merchandising (organic, prepared produce, premium deli), monitor stock availability on high-movers.
Rebalance packaged vs. fresh bakery mix: investigate cannibalisation, test promotional support on fresh bakery to drive margin recovery.

leaks.title: Beers/Wines margin collapse at Palmerstown
leaks.fix: Review promotional calendar: validate if price reductions are driving volume or eroding margin. Audit supplier terms and shrink. Reset price points using Skerries (+0.2% holding) as benchmark. Consider tiered promotion (one SKU deep, others at target margin).

leaks.title: Packaged bakery systematic decline (all stores)
leaks.fix: Investigate shelf conflict with fresh bakery: test moving packaged to impulse zone in Palmerstown/Balbriggan. Check expiry/freshness at point of sale. Review promotional support: if scratch bakery is being heavily promoted, shift budget to packaged to balance mix. Check competitor pricing in Dublin/North Dublin.

leaks.title: Fish serveover rationalisation trend (Skerries -23.5%)
leaks.fix: Validate staffing coverage at deli counter during peak hours. Review counter visibility and merchandise facing. Test cross-merchandising with deli sandwiches or hot food (bundled offers). Confirm supply consistency and product quality feedback from customers.

leaks.title: Balbriggan impulse softness (-3.8% weekly, -1.85% YTD)
leaks.fix: Audit impulse planogram: compare against Skerries (performing +12.2%). Check in-stock position on top sellers (water, soft drinks, confectionery). Review POS placement and promotional visibility. Investigate if Balbriggan is losing footfall to competing discount retailers or online; validate basket composition.

leaks.title: Dairy waste at Balbriggan elevated (1.34% vs. 1.21% Palmerstown, 1.11% Skerries)
leaks.fix: Review ordering/intake procedures and shelf rotation discipline. Audit temperature and storage conditions. Cross-check against waste detail by category (high-impact items: S0001 confectionery 3.27% waste at Balbriggan vs. best practice). Implement daily shrink huddles on dairy/impulse.

urgent.text: Balbriggan: Initiate emergency diagnostic visit this week. Review promotional calendar, supply confirms, and staff engagement. Compare planograms line-by-line to Skerries. Validate if external factors (local competition, road works, school holidays) are depressing footfall.

Margin erosion: Convene commercial + ops call to review promotional intensity and pack down aggressive discounting. Validate Q3 trade calendar is not front-loading summer promotions. Establish margin floor by department and enforce promotional approval workflow.

today: Pull Balbriggan YoY detailed comparison for same week 2025 (Easter shifts may be skewing perception; Easter 2025 was 20 April vs 2024 date). Check if comparable is distorted by shift.
Audit fish serveover staffing roster across all three stores for weeks 24-26; validate cover during 11am-2pm and 5pm-7pm peaks. Cross-reference with sales patterns.
Request supply confirmations from Meat/Fish suppliers: any allocation issues, quality holds, or delivery timing delays impacting Balbriggan or Skerries?

Produce team: flag Skerries organic momentum to buying; validate supply pipeline can sustain +45.9% growth. Monitor shelf stock on bananas (932.6 units top seller), salads (637 units, high margin €617).
Bakery: schedule in-store visits to all three stores; visually inspect packaged vs. fresh shelf positioning, expiry management, and promotional signage. Document photos.

Deli: confirm hot food equipment functionality and staffing scheduling, especially Palmerstown (+27.4%) and Balbriggan (+13.5%); validate if hot food success is masking other deli weaknesses (sandwich -10.7% at Palmerstown).

compliance.text: Waste data integrity: Balbriggan dairy waste reporting shows discrepancies (S0001 impulse confectionery 3.27% waste vs. all-store average ~1-2%). Audit EPOS waste codes and counting procedure to ensure accurate P&L reflection.

Margin variance: Palmerstown meat margin -4.07% (category margin 16.67%) is material. Validate whether mark-down/reduction activity is being coded correctly and captured in waste vs. margin calculations. Risk of profit leakage if not tracked.

cross.title: Deli-Produce synergy opportunity at Skerries
cross.why: Prepared produce +12.1% and deli sandwiches +21.8% suggest strong occasion-based eating, likely driven by tourists/affluent daytime traffic. Opportunity to bundle and upsell across categories.
cross.action: Test bundled 'lunch packs' at Skerries deli counter pairing sandwiches + fresh salads + drinks. Track attachment rate weekly. If successful (target +5% incremental), roll to Palmerstown (lunchtime commuter market).

cross.title: Seasonal fresh bakery vs. packaged bakery rebalance
cross.why: Fresh bakery performing well (+15.7%, +22.5%, +3.9% across stores) while packaged collapses (-25.7%, -27.4%, -2.9%), but scratch bakery margins are strong (50.5%-52.8%). Mix is moving positive but SKU rationalisation risk exists.
cross.action: Work with bakery ops to reduce packaged SKU count by 20% (consolidate slow lines, promote faster-moving fresh alternatives). Reallocate shelf space to fresh. Target: improve packaged turns, reduce waste, maintain margin via fresh upside.

cross.title: Impulse+Beers/Wines margin crisis connection
cross.why: Beers/Wines down -7.5% at Palmerstown and flat at Skerries, with margin compression across all stores. Impulse also weak at Balbriggan. Suggests promotional overlap or category misalignment in merchandising strategy.
cross.action: Audit promotional calendar for Q2/Q3: identify any 'category stacking' (simultaneous promos on beer + soft drinks + snacks). Reset quarterly trade calendar with staggered promos. Test 'drink bundling' (beer + snacks) to drive attachment and margin recovery.

cross.title: Balbriggan personal care decline (-12.1%) vs. Skerries stability (-11.3% YTD)
cross.why: Balbriggan personal care suffering despite comparable store type; Skerries holding better suggests execution/merchandising gap, not market issue.
cross.action: Benchmark planogram, promotional support, and staff engagement at personal care in Skerries vs. Balbriggan. Validate in-stock position on top SKUs (health/beauty/baby). If Balbriggan is short-stocked or misplaced, restock and remerchandise this week.

diagnosis.wrong: Balbriggan is in distress across nearly all major categories (impulse, edible, non-food, personal care, baby/kids all negative YTD), while Skerries and Palmerstown show pockets of strength. This is not a market downturn—it is a store-specific execution problem.
diagnosis.why: Root causes are likely multifactorial: (1) Promotional calendar misalignment or under-investment vs. peers; (2) Supply/availability issues (implicit in weak ranges like fish, lamb, pork); (3) Staffing or morale gaps impacting merchandising and customer experience; (4) Potential loss of market share to local discounters or online; (5) Possible construction/road works or local event disruption affecting footfall. The fact that deli and fresh bakery are performing suggests the store is not broken operationally, but core grocery basket is leaking.
diagnosis.fix: Immediate actions: (1) Visit Balbriggan on-site this week with regional/head office buying and ops team; conduct full planogram audit against Skerries; validate top 50 SKU in-stock position and shelf presence. (2) Pull YoY week comparison to Easter shift (confirm same week 2025 baseline); if comparable is distorted, reset expectations. (3) Audit promotional calendar for last 8 weeks and next 8 weeks: identify any gaps vs. Palmerstown/Skerries; realign Q3 support. (4) Conduct staff pulse survey at Balbriggan deli/grocery area; flag morale/scheduling concerns to store manager. (5) Request supply confirmations from all major suppliers (Meat, Produce, Grocery Edible, Personal Care) to rule out allocation/delivery issues. (6) Implement daily sales + margin dashboards (vs. plan and vs. Skerries) for 4 weeks; weekly review calls with store leadership to drive accountability. Target: +3-4% sales recovery by week 30.

waste.summary: Across the Moriarty Group stores, total waste for Q2 2026 reaches approximately €2,847 across Palmerstown and Skerries (Balbriggan data unavailable). Palmerstown generated €1,028 in waste across 50 product lines, driven primarily by ready-to-eat meals, deli items, and fresh produce with poor sell-through. Skerries experienced €1,819 in waste, significantly impacted by premium ready-meals and specialty products with severe ordering misalignment—notably Happy Pear energy balls (24 units wasted, only 2 sold) and Heinz baked beans (37 units wasted vs. 27 sold). Critical patterns emerge: high-waste items with minimal retail sales indicate systematic ordering problems, while products requiring heavy markdowns suggest pricing or shelf-life issues.

stores.name: Palmerstown

top_offenders.insight: Critical ordering failure: 14 units wasted with only 12 sold at full price. Heavy markdown dependency (8 units reduced) suggests poor demand forecasting or overstock strategy. Recommend reducing order frequency and quantities by 40% until demand stabilizes.

top_offenders.product: SV LAMB LOIN CHOPS FAM VAL 390GRM
top_offenders.insight: Meat category waste: 8 units wasted with minimal sales (9 units). No reductions applied suggests shelf-life expiry rather than pricing issues. Implement shorter lead times and daily rather than weekly ordering for this premium cut.

Deli/prepared food waste with high markdown dependency: 12 of 13 wasted units required price reductions. Sales (19) don't justify production volumes. Recommend production cut of 30% and validate whether this item aligns with store traffic patterns.

Produce waste: 7 units wasted versus 3 sold—extreme mismatch indicating over-buying or quality issues. No reductions applied suggests rapid deterioration. Reduce order to 4 units per replenishment and monitor fruit quality on receipt.

Prepared foods with markdown reliance: all 14 wasted units reduced, though retail sales (78) are healthy. Suggests production scheduling misalignment with demand peaks. Shift to on-demand or 4-hour production cycles rather than daily batches.

stores.category_breakdown: Prepared foods/deli (hot chicken, spicy wedges, sausage rolls) drive 35% of waste through over-production without demand validation. Meat/protein (lamb chops, chicken breast, beef stir-fry) accounts for 28% due to short shelf-life and over-ordering of premium cuts. Produce (apples, peppers, spinach, lettuce) represents 22% with quality-related spoilage. Dairy/other (butter) 15%. Root cause: daily production/ordering without real-time sales data integration.
stores.action: Immediately audit Palmerstown deli production schedules and link to hourly POS data. Reduce prepared-food batch sizes by 40% and implement 4-hour replenishment cycles. For premium meats (lamb, beef), switch from standing orders to daily order-by-demand model with supplier confirmation same-morning. Enforce quality inspections on produce receipt and reduce apple bag orders to 50% of current levels until demand improves.

stores.name: Balbriggan
stores.category_breakdown: No waste data available for analysis. Balbriggan (Store 374) did not submit Q2 waste reporting.
stores.action: Immediate investigation required: confirm whether waste data collection system is operational at Balbriggan. If data collection is inactive, activate immediately and backfill Q2 records. If store has genuinely zero waste, identify and document best practices for replication across group.

stores.name: Skerries

top_offenders.product: HAPPY PEAR PEANUT & CRAN ENERGY BAL 125GRM
top_offenders.insight: Catastrophic ordering failure: 24 units wasted with only 2 sold—1,100% waste-to-sales ratio. Premium product with zero reductions suggests expiry-driven waste not pricing. Immediately cease automatic replenishment; move to manual ordering with confirmed demand.

top_offenders.product: HAPPY PEAR CHOC DATE CAR ENE BALLS 125GRM
top_offenders.insight: Same supplier/category as above: 17 units wasted vs. 8 sold. Pattern suggests Happy Pear products are not resonating with Skerries demographics. Remove entire range from auto-order and test single unit placement weekly only.

Staple product with unexpected waste: 37 units wasted vs. 27 sold suggests either packaging damage, shelf damage, or overstock beyond shelf-life window. Investigate warehouse storage; implement FIFO rotation discipline and reduce case pack ordering for this SKU.

Extreme markdown dependency: 56 units reduced (37% of sales) with 14 wasted. Despite healthy retail sales (94), heavy discounting indicates over-production. Reduce batch sizes by 30% and implement markdown thresholds to trigger waste reduction alerts.

top_offenders.product: HAPPY PEAR MUSHROOM & SWEET POT SOUP 400GRM
top_offenders.insight: Premium prepared meal: 11 units wasted vs. 1 sold—1,000% waste-to-sales ratio. Identical pattern to energy balls. Confirm whether Skerries customer base supports premium health foods or if product assortment is misaligned with store demographics.

stores.category_breakdown: Premium ready-meals/health foods (Happy Pear energy balls, soups, risottos) drive 31% of waste through demand forecasting failures—these products show 5-15x waste-to-sales ratios suggesting demographic/pricing mismatch. Prepared foods and ready-meals (signature dishes, souffle, salmon) account for 28% with markdown reliance. Produce (peppers, lettuce, lettuce bags) 18% with modest waste-to-sales ratios suggesting acceptable stock management. Packaged goods (baked beans, biscuits, dairy) 23%. Root cause: Skerries appears to stock premium/specialty brands without validating local customer demand.
stores.action: Conduct urgent customer demographic and basket analysis for Skerries: premium health food penetration is negligible (Happy Pear waste ratios unacceptable). Immediately pause all Happy Pear auto-replenishment and replace with locally-validated alternatives. For all ready-meal categories, reduce order quantities by 35% and implement 3-day sell-through targets with automated reorder triggers. Implement daily waste reporting (not quarterly) to catch ordering failures in real-time.

group_actions: Establish group-wide POS-to-ordering system integration: all stores must link daily sales velocity to replenishment orders within 5 business days. Palmerstown and Skerries show patterns of batch-and-forget ordering (e.g., weekly deli production, standing orders for meats) disconnected from actual demand.

Implement 24-hour waste escalation protocol: any SKU exceeding 10 units waste in single week or showing waste-to-retail-sales ratio >50% triggers automatic order freeze and manager investigation. Balbriggan's missing data suggests systematic reporting gaps—audit all stores' waste logging processes monthly.

Create store-level product assortment validation process: Skerries premium product range (Happy Pear, specialty meats, deli items) generates 77% higher waste than retail sales on average. Implement quarterly basket analysis by store demographics and remove underperforming SKUs. Standardize prepared-food production to 4-hour batch cycles (not daily) with real-time demand signals.
